entries.name: Rotate Function
entries.description: Rotates x the number of bits specified by y. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x rotated left by y
terminals.description: x rotated left by y is the result of the rotation. The data type of x rotated left by y is determined by the data type of the x input. The following table illustrates how x and y affect x rotated left by y: y x x rotated left by y Comments 1 3 (00000011) 6 (00000110) This function shifts the first one bit on the left, 0, to the right end. 2 3 (00000011) 12 (00001100) This function shifts the first two bits on the left, 00, to the right end. –3 3 (00000011) 96 (01100000) This function shifts the

entries.name: Split Number Function
entries.description: Breaks a number into its component bytes or words.

terminals.name: hi(x)
terminals.description: hi(x) and lo(x) are integers that are half the width of x. hi(x) and lo(x) are 8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit Functions 562 ni.com unsigned integers, respectively, or an array or cluster of those representations. hi(x) is the numerically high-order byte and lo(x) is the numerically low-order byte, regardless of the endianness of the operating system.

terminals.name: lo(x)
terminals.description: If you wire an 8-bit integer input, this function does not split the number. Instead, LabVIEW returns the same 8-bit number in the lo(x) output, and returns zero for the hi(x) output.

entries.name: Join Numbers Function
entries.description: Creates a number from the component bytes or words. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: hi
terminals.description: hi can be an 8-, 16-, or 32-bit number or an array or cluster of that representation.

terminals.name: lo
terminals.description: lo can be an 8-, 16-, or 32-bit number or an array or cluster of that representation.

terminals.name: (hi.lo)
terminals.description: (hi.lo) is an integer twice the width of hi and lo. (hi.lo) is a 16-, 32-, or 64-bit unsigned integer or an array or cluster of those representations. If hi and lo are of different widths, (hi.lo) is twice the width of the widest number.

entries.name: Swap Bytes Function
entries.description: Swaps the high-order 8 bits and the low-order 8 bits for every word in data. Functions © National Instruments 563 If you want to byte-swap floating-point or fixed-point numbers, use the Type Cast function to cast the value to a 1D array of bytes by wiring an array of numeric controls to the type input and the floating-point or fixed-point numbers to the x input. Then use the Reverse 1D Array function to convert the array to a floating-point number with the elements reversed. The connector pane d

terminals.name: data
terminals.description: data is an integer, an array of integers, or a cluster containing integers that you want to byte swap. If data is a cluster that contains integers, this function swaps only the integer elements of the cluster. Neither refnums or variants are valid inputs. Both will break the input wire.

terminals.name: byte swapped
terminals.description: byte swapped is of the same data type and structure as data.

entries.name: Swap Words Function
entries.description: Swaps the high-order 16 bits and the low-order 16 bits for every long integer in data. For example, if the input in hexadecimal format is 1234567890ABCDEF, this function swaps 1234 with 5678 and 90AB with CDEF, returning 56781234CDEF90AB. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: data
terminals.description: Functions 564 ni.com data is an integer, an array of integers, or a cluster containing integers that you want to word swap. In the case of a cluster that contains integers, this function swaps only the integer elements of the cluster. If you want to byte-swap floating-point or fixed-point numbers, use the Type Cast function to cast the value to a 1D array of bytes. Then use the Reverse 1D Array function and type cast it back to a floating-point or fixed-point number. Neither refnums or variants

terminals.name: word swapped
terminals.description: word swapped is of the same data type and structure as data.

entries.name: Byte Array Checksum
entries.description: Computes the message digest on a byte array. You must manually select the polymorphic instance you want to use. • SHA-256 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA-224 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA-384 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA-512 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA-512-256 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA-512-224 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA3-224 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA3-256 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA3-384 Byte Array Checksum VI • SHA3-512 Byte Array Checksum VI SHA-256 Byte Array Checksum VI SHA-256 Byte Ar

terminals.name: buffer
terminals.description: buffer specifies the byte array, or buffer, for which you want to compute the message digest.

terminals.name: digest
terminals.description: digest returns the SHA-256 message digest of the byte array, or buffer, as a hexadecimal string.

entries.name: Absolute Value Function
entries.description: Returns the absolute value of the input. Functions 570 ni.com The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: abs(x)
terminals.description: abs(x) is the absolute value of x. When x is of the form x = a + bi, that is, when x is complex, the following equation defines abs(x): When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the function. The resulting VI has the same icon but contains a matrix-specific algorithm. The node remains a VI if you disconnect the matrix from the inputs. Wire other data types as inputs to restore the original function. If you wire

entries.name: Round To Nearest Function
entries.description: Rounds the input to the nearest integer. If the value of the input is midway between two integers, the function returns the nearest even integer. For example, if number is 1.5 or 2.5, nearest integer value is 2. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: nearest integer value
terminals.description: nearest integer value is the resulting nearest integer to number. If the input is a time stamp value, the function rounds to the nearest second. When number is in the form of a + bi, that is, when number is complex, the function returns a complex number defined by the nearest integer to the real and imaginary parts of number. The following equation defines nearest integer value: nearest integer value(x) = nearest integer value(a) + i * nearest integer value(b) Examples Refer to the following exa

entries.name: Round Toward -Infinity Function
entries.description: Truncates the input to the next lowest integer. For example, if the input is 3.8, the result is 3. If the input is –3.8, the result is –4. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: floor(x): largest int <= x
terminals.description: floor(x): largest int ≤ x is the resulting lowest integer, closest to x. If the input is a time stamp value, the function rounds to the previous second. When x is of the form x = a + bi, that is, when x is complex, the function returns a complex number defined by the next lowest integers to the real and imaginary parts of x. The following equation defines floor(x): largest int ≤ x: floor(x) = floor(a) + i floor(b) Round Toward +Infinity Function Round Toward +Infinity Function Rounds the input t
terminals.default_value: data types for this polymorphic function

entries.name: Round Toward +Infinity Function
entries.description: Rounds the input to the next highest integer. For example, if the input is 3.1, the result is 4. If the input is –3.1, the result is –3. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: ceil(x): smallest int >= x
terminals.description: ceil(x): smallest int >= x is the resulting highest integer, closest to x. If the input is a time stamp value, the function rounds to the next second. When x is of the form x = a + bi, that is, when x is complex, the function returns a complex number defined by the next highest integers to the real and imaginary parts of x. The following equation defines ceil(x): smallest int >= x: ceil(x) = ceil(a) + i ceil(b)

entries.name: Scale By Power Of 2 Function
entries.description: Multiplies x by 2 raised to the power of n. If x is an integer or fixed-point number, this function is the equivalent of an arithmetic shift. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x*2^n
terminals.description: x*2^n is the result of multiplying x by 2, raised to the power of n. If you wire a fixed-point value to this function, the resulting output value retains the Functions 574 ni.com fixed-point configuration settings of the input value. However, the value is likely to truncate or wrap. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Complex Conjugate Function
entries.description: Produces the complex conjugate of x + iy. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x + iy
terminals.description: x + iy can be a complex number, array or cluster of complex numbers, an array of cluster of complex numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: x - iy
terminals.description: x - iy is the complex conjugate of x + iy.

entries.name: Polar To Complex Function
entries.description: Creates a complex number from two values in polar notation. The dimensions of two matrices that you want to combine must be the same. Otherwise, this function returns an empty complex matrix. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: theta
terminals.description: Functions 576 ni.com theta can be a scalar number, an array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on. theta is in radians.

terminals.name: r * e^(i*theta)
terminals.description: r * e^(i*theta) is of the same data type structure as r and theta, with complex representation instead of scalar. Given z in rectangular form z = a + bi, this function converts the polar components according to the following equation: z = r*cos(theta) + i r*sin(theta) When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the function. The resulting VI has the same icon but contains a matrix-specific algorithm. The node rema

entries.name: Complex To Polar Function
entries.description: Breaks a complex number into its polar components. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: r * e^(i*theta)
terminals.description: r * e^(i*theta) can be a complex number, a cluster of complex numbers, an array of complex numbers, an array of clusters of complex numbers, and so on.

terminals.name: theta
terminals.description: theta is of the same data type structure as r * e^(i*theta), with scalar representation instead of complex. theta is in radians. Given z in rectangular form z = a + bi, this function converts the polar components of z = r * e^(i*theta) according to the following equations: r = |z| = sqrt(a² + b²) theta = arg(z) = arctan2(b,a) When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the function. The resulting VI has the same i

entries.name: Re/Im To Complex Function
entries.description: Creates a complex number from two values in rectangular notation. The dimensions of two matrices that you want to combine must be the same. Otherwise, this function returns an empty complex matrix. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x + iy
terminals.description: x + iy has the same structure as the input, with complex representation instead of scalar. When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the function. The resulting VI has the same icon but contains a matrix-specific algorithm. The node remains a VI if you disconnect the matrix from the input(s). Wire other data types as inputs to restore the original function. If you wire a data type to a function and that data typ

entries.name: Complex To Re/Im Function
entries.description: Breaks a complex number into its rectangular components. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x + iy
terminals.description: x + iy can be a complex number, array or cluster of complex numbers, an array of cluster of complex numbers, and so on.

entries.name: Re/Im To Polar Function
entries.description: Converts the rectangular components of a complex number into its polar components. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: theta
terminals.description: theta is of the same data type structure as x and y. theta is in radians. This function converts the rectangular components to polar components using the following equations: r = sqrt(x² + y²) theta = arctan2(y,x) radians Functions 580 ni.com

entries.name: Polar To Re/Im Function
entries.description: Converts the polar components of a complex number into its rectangular components.

terminals.name: theta
terminals.description: theta can be a scalar number, an array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on. theta is in radians.

entries.name: Square Root Function
entries.description: Computes the square root of the input value. If x is negative, the square root is NaN unless x is complex. If x is a matrix, this function takes the matrix square root of x. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions © National Instruments 581

terminals.name: sqrt(x)
terminals.description: sqrt(x) is a double-precision, floating-point number if x is an integer. If x is less than 0, sqrt(x) is not a number (NaN), unless x is complex. When x is of the form x = a + bi, that is, when x is complex, the function defines the magnitude and phase with the following equations: where |x| is the magnitude of x and arg(x) is the phase of x: arg(x) = arctan2(b,a) When you wire matrix data as an input to this function, a VI that includes subVIs that work with the matrix data type replaces the fu

entries.name: Square Function
entries.description: Computes the square of the input value. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x^2
terminals.description: x^2 is of the same numeric representation as x. Note You can manually configure this function to output data of a type you want. To specify the output data type, right-click the function and select Properties to display Functions © National Instruments 583 If you wire fixed-point values to Numeric functions such as Add, Subtract, Multiply, and Square, the functions usually return values that do not lose any bits of word length. However, if the operation creates a value that exceeds the maximum w

entries.name: Negate Function
entries.description: Negates the input value. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: -x
terminals.description: the Object Properties dialog box. On the Output Configuration page, click the Representation icon and select the data type you want. A blue coercion dot appears on the output terminal of the function to indicate that you have configured the output data type. Functions 584 ni.com -x is the negative value of x. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Reciprocal Function
entries.description: Divides 1 by the input value. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: 1/x
terminals.description: 1/x is infinity if x is 0. If x is an integer, 1/x is a double-precision, floating-point number. Fixed-Point Details If you wire a fixed-point value to this function, by default LabVIEW configures the integer word length of the reciprocal to avoid overflow. However, because the precision of the reciprocal can be infinite, rounding conditions always occur. Use the Numeric Node Properties dialog box to configure how LabVIEW handles rounding of fixed-point data. This function always uses the Satura
terminals.default_value: LabVIEW configures the integer word length of the reciprocal to avoid overflow

entries.name: Sign Function
entries.description: Returns the sign of number. Text-based programming languages typically call this function signum or sgn. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a scalar number, array or cluster of numbers, array of clusters of numbers, and so on.

–1, 0, 1 returns 1 if the input value is greater than 0, returns 0 if the input value is equal to 0, and returns –1 if the input value is less than 0. When number is in the form of a + bi, that is, when number is complex, the following defines -1, 0, 1: Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW. Note For nonzero complex numbers, this function returns a complex value with the same phase as the input and with a magnitude of 1. Functions 586 ni.com

entries.name: Convert RTD Reading
entries.description: Converts a voltage you read from an RTD into temperature in Celsius. Wire data to the RTDvolts input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. • Convert RTD Reading (waveform) VI • Convert RTD Reading (scaler) VI Convert RTD Reading (waveform) VI Convert RTD Reading (waveform) VI Converts a voltage you read from an RTD into temperature in Celsius. Wire data to the RTDvolts input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance.

terminals.name: Ro
terminals.description: Functions 588 ni.com Ro is the RTD resistance at 0° C. The default is 100 Ω.

terminals.name: RTDvolts
terminals.description: RTDvolts is the voltage you read from the RTD.

terminals.name: Iex
terminals.description: Iex is the excitation current you used with the RTD. This parameter defaults to an excitation current of 0.15 mA.

terminals.name: RTDtemp
terminals.description: RTDtemp is the return temperature value in degrees Celsius. Convert RTD Reading (scaler) VI Convert RTD Reading (scaler) VI Converts a voltage you read from an RTD into temperature in Celsius. Wire data to the RTDvolts input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. Inputs/Outputs

entries.name: Convert Strain Gauge Reading
entries.description: Converts a voltage read from a strain gauge to units of strain. Wire data to the Vsg input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. • Convert Strain Gauge Reading (waveform) VI • Convert Strain Gauge Reading (scaler) VI The conversion formula the VI uses is based solely on the bridge configuration. The following illustrations show the seven bridge configurations you can use and the corresponding formulas. For all bridge configurations, the VI uses the followi

entries.name: Convert Thermistor Reading
entries.description: Converts a thermistor voltage into a temperature. This VI has different modes of operation for voltage-excited and current-excited thermistors. Wire data to the Voltage input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. • Convert Thermistor Reading (waveform) VI • Convert Thermistor Reading (scaler) VI The Convert Thermistor Reading VI has two modes of operation for use with different types of thermistor circuits. The following illustration shows how the thermist

terminals.name: Type of Excitation
terminals.description: Type of Excitation distinguishes the two modes of operation of the VI. This VI defaults to the Voltage Reference mode. Use the Voltage Reference (VREF) and the R1 values only in this mode. You use the Current Excitation (IEX) value only in the Current Reference mode. This input accepts the following values. 0 Voltage Reference 1 Current Reference

terminals.name: Voltage
terminals.description: Voltage is the voltage read from a thermistor.

terminals.name: Voltage Reference
terminals.description: Voltage Reference (VREF) is the reference you apply across a resistor of known value in series with your thermistor. The default is 2.5 V. Use this input only when you set Type of Excitation to Voltage Reference.

terminals.name: R1
terminals.description: R1 is the value of the resistor in series with your thermistor expressed in Ohms. The default is 5 kΩ. Use this input only when you set Type of Excitation to Voltage Reference.

terminals.name: Excitation Current
terminals.description: Excitation Current (IEX) is the current excitation applied to the thermistor. The default is 100 µA. Use this input only when you set Type of Excitation to Current Reference.

terminals.name: Temperature Units (C)
terminals.description: Temperature Units is the units of temperature the VI outputs. This input accepts the following values. 0 Celsius 1 Fahrenheit 2 Kelvin Functions

entries.name: Convert Thermocouple Reading
entries.description: Converts a voltage reading from a thermocouple into a temperature value. Wire data to the Voltage Waveform (waveform), Voltage Array (array), or Thermocouple Voltage (scalar) input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. • Convert Thermocouple Reading (waveform) VI • Convert Thermocouple Reading (array) VI • Convert Thermocouple Reading (scaler) VI Convert Thermocouple Reading (waveform) Convert Thermocouple Reading (waveform) VI VI Converts a voltage readin

terminals.name: Temperature Units (C)
terminals.description: Temperature Units is the units of temperature the VI outputs. This input accepts the following values. 0 Celsius 1 Fahrenheit 2 Kelvin 3 Rankine

terminals.name: Voltage Waveform
terminals.description: Voltage Waveform is a waveform of voltages read from a thermocouple.

terminals.name: ThermocoupleType
terminals.description: ThermocoupleType can be B, E, J, K, R, S, T, or N. This input accepts the following values. Refer to your thermocouple user manual for more information about thermocouple types. 0 B 1 E Note This VI does not work with the NI 9211 C Series module. Instead, use the NI 9211 Convert to Temperature VI located in the labview\examples\ CompactRIO\Module Specific\NI 9211\NI 9211 Support Files.llb directory. Functions

entries.name: Radians to Degrees
entries.description: Converts data from radians to degrees. This VI is a preconfigured Expression Node. Functions 614 ni.com

entries.name: Degrees to Radians
entries.description: Converts data from degrees to radians. This VI is a preconfigured Expression Node.

entries.name: Radians per Second to RPM
entries.description: Converts data from radians per second to revolutions per minute (RPM). This VI is a preconfigured Expression Node.

entries.name: Random Number (0-1) Function
entries.description: Produces a double-precision, floating-point number between 0 and 1. The number generated is greater than or equal to 0, but less than 1. The distribution is uniform. Alternatively, you can use several of the Signal Generation VIs or the Signal Generation PtByPt VIs to regenerate the same random sequence. For example, the Uniform White Noise VI allows you to set a seed number that you can use to initialize the generation of a pseudorandom pattern.

terminals.name: number (0 to 1)
terminals.description: number (0 to 1) is a double-precision, floating-point number between 0 and 1. Random Number (Range) Random Number (Range) Generates a random value from a specified range. Wire data to the upper bound or lower bound input to determine the polymorphic instance to use or manually select the instance. Functions 618 ni.com

terminals.name: upper bound (100)
terminals.description: upper bound specifies the upper limit of the range. The default is 100.

terminals.name: lower bound (0)
terminals.description: lower bound specifies the lower limit of the range. The default is 0.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number returns a random number greater than or equal to lower bound and less than or equal to upper bound. If upper bound is less than or equal to lower bound, this output returns lower bound. Random Number (Range) I64 VI Random Number (Range) I64 VI Generates a random value from a specified range. entries.name: Fixed-Point to Integer Cast Function
entries.description: Scales the fixed-point input to produce an integer output. This function is the equivalent of an arithmetic shift of the input, constrained so that the least significant bit of the input becomes the least significant bit of the output.

terminals.name: fixed-point
terminals.description: fixed-point is the data you want to scale. fixed-point can be any fixed-point data type.

terminals.name: integer
terminals.description: integer is the input data scaled to the smallest compatible integer type that prevents overflow. This output discards the overflow status. If fixed-point is a signed number, LabVIEW sign-extends the input data as necessary to ensure that the input and output data are always the same sign. Examples Refer to the following example files included with LabVIEW.

entries.name: Integer to Fixed-Point Cast Function
entries.description: Scales the integer input to produce an output with the requested fixed-point type. This function is the equivalent of an arithmetic shift of the input with configurable overflow handling, constrained so that the least significant bit of the input becomes the least significant bit of the output.

terminals.name: fixed-point type
terminals.description: fixed-point type is the fixed-point data type to which you want to convert the integer data. You can configure the fixed-point data type in the Data Type page of the Properties dialog box. LabVIEW ignores any data in the constant or control that you wire to fixed-point type. You also can leave this input unwired and configure the output data type in the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box.

terminals.name: integer
terminals.description: integer is the data you want to scale. integer can be any integer data type.

terminals.name: fixed-point
terminals.description: fixed-point is the input data scaled to the requested fixed-point data type. If the word length of integer is greater than or less than the word length of fixed-point type, LabVIEW first converts integer to the integer word length of fixed-point type before casting integer to the requested fixed-point type. The least significant bit of integer becomes the least significant bit of fixed-point, so rounding is never necessary. To override the default behavior of this function, right-click the funct
terminals.default_value: behavior of this function, right-click the function and select Properties from the shortcut menu to display the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box

entries.name: Clear Fixed-Point Overflow Status Function
entries.description: Clears the overflow status of FXP by changing the overflow status of FXP to FALSE.

terminals.name: FXP
terminals.description: FXP specifies a fixed-point number.

terminals.name: FXP with overflow cleared
terminals.description: FXP with overflow cleared returns FXP with an overflow status of FALSE. If FXP does not include an overflow status, FXP with overflow cleared returns FXP unchanged.

terminals.name: overflow?
terminals.description: overflow? is TRUE if FXP includes an overflow status and FXP is the result of an operation that overflowed. If FXP does not include an overflow status, or if FXP includes an overflow status but is not the result of an operation that overflowed, overflow? is FALSE. Note The Integer to Fixed-Point Cast function combines elements of the Conversion functions, the Type Cast function, and the Scale by Power of 2 function. The Integer to Fixed-Point Cast function performs an operation that reinterprets

entries.name: Remove Fixed-Point Overflow Status Function
entries.description: Removes the overflow status from FXP. To change the overflow status to FALSE, use the Clear Fixed-Point Overflow Status function.

terminals.name: FXP
terminals.description: FXP specifies a fixed-point number.

terminals.name: FXP with overflow removed
terminals.description: FXP with overflow removed returns FXP without an overflow status. If FXP does not include an overflow status, FXP with overflow removed returns FXP unchanged.

terminals.name: overflow?
terminals.description: overflow? is TRUE if FXP includes an overflow status and FXP is the result of an operation that overflowed. If FXP does not include an overflow status, or if FXP includes an overflow status but is not the result of an operation that overflowed, overflow? is FALSE.

entries.name: Include Fixed-Point Overflow Status Function
entries.description: Includes an overflow status with FXP. The value of the overflow status equals the value specified in overflow.

terminals.name: FXP
terminals.description: Functions © National Instruments 625 FXP specifies a fixed-point number.

terminals.name: overflow
terminals.description: overflow specifies whether the overflow status of FXP with overflow included is TRUE or FALSE.

terminals.name: FXP with overflow included
terminals.description: FXP with overflow included returns FXP with an overflow status set to the value specified in overflow.

entries.name: Positive Infinity
entries.description: Returns the value Inf (infinity). LabVIEW converts Inf to the highest value for a data type. For example, converting Inf Functions 626 ni.com to a 16-bit signed integer returns the value 32,767, the highest possible value for a 16-bit signed integer. Machine Epsilon Machine Epsilon Represents the round-off error for a floating-point number with a given precision. Use the machine epsilon constant to compare whether two floating-point numbers are equivalent. Not A Number Constant Not A Number Cons

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type specifies the data type. This input accepts any scalar numeric data type and enum.

terminals.name: maximum value for type
terminals.description: Functions 628 ni.com

entries.name: Machine Epsilon
entries.description: Represents the round-off error for a floating-point number with a given precision. Use the machine epsilon constant to compare whether two floating-point numbers are equivalent. Not A Number Constant Not A Number Constant Returns the value NaN (not a number). You can change the numeric representation of this constant by right-clicking the constant and selecting Representation from the shortcut menu. You also can assign a label to this constant. Math & Scientific Constants Math & Scientific Const

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type specifies the data type. This input accepts any scalar numeric data type and enum.

terminals.name: maximum value for type
terminals.description: Functions 628 ni.com maximum value for type returns the maximum value of the data type wired to type. If type is an enum, this VI returns the last value of the enum. For example, if type is a 16-bit unsigned integer, this output returns 32767. If type is an enum with values {cat, dog, bird}, this output returns bird.

terminals.name: minimum value for type
terminals.description: minimum value for type returns the minimum value of the data type wired to type. If type is an enum, this VI returns the first value of the enum. For example, if type is a 16-bit unsigned integer, this output returns -32768. If type is an enum with values {cat, dog, bird}, this output returns cat.

entries.name: Range Limits for Type
entries.description: Returns the maximum and minimum values of the input data type.

terminals.name: type
terminals.description: type specifies the data type. This input accepts any scalar numeric data type and enum.

terminals.name: maximum value for type
terminals.description: Functions 628 ni.com maximum value for type returns the maximum value of the data type wired to type. If type is an enum, this VI returns the last value of the enum. For example, if type is a 16-bit unsigned integer, this output returns 32767. If type is an enum with values {cat, dog, bird}, this output returns bird.

terminals.name: minimum value for type
terminals.description: minimum value for type returns the minimum value of the data type wired to type. If type is an enum, this VI returns the first value of the enum. For example, if type is a 16-bit unsigned integer, this output returns -32768. If type is an enum with values {cat, dog, bird}, this output returns cat.

entries.name: Expression Node
entries.description: Use the Expression Node to calculate expressions that contain a single variable. The following built-in functions are allowed in formulas: abs, acos, acosh, asin, asinh, atan, atanh, ceil, cos, cosh, cot, csc, exp, expm1, floor, getexp, getman, int, intrz, ln, lnp1, log, log2, max, min, mod, rand, rem, sec, sign, sin, sinc, sinh, sizeOfDim, sqrt, tan, tanh.

terminals.name: Expression Nodes accept any non-complex numeric data type.
Expression Nodes accept only the period (.) as a decimal separator. The nodes do
Expression Nodes accept only pi as a constant. The constant is case-sensitive.
You cannot add Expression Nodes to a VI on which you enable inlining.

entries.name: And Function
entries.description: Computes the logical AND of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are TRUE, the function returns TRUE. Otherwise, it returns FALSE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x .and. y?
terminals.description: x .and. y? is the logical AND of x and y. And Truth Table x y x .and. y?

entries.name: Or Function
entries.description: Computes the logical OR of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are FALSE, the function returns FALSE. Otherwise, it returns TRUE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

entries.name: Exclusive Or Function
entries.description: Computes the logical exclusive or (XOR) of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are TRUE or both inputs are FALSE, the function returns FALSE. Otherwise, it returns TRUE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x .xor. y?
terminals.description: x .xor. y? is the logical exclusive or (XOR) of x and y. Exclusive Or Truth Table x y x .xor. y?

entries.name: Not Function
entries.description: Computes the logical negation of the input. If x is FALSE, the function returns TRUE. If x is TRUE, the function returns FALSE. Note This function performs bitwise operations on numeric inputs. Functions © National Instruments 635 The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: .not. x?
terminals.description: .not. x? is the logical negation of x. Not Truth Table x .not. x? F

entries.name: Not And Function
entries.description: Computes the logical NAND of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are TRUE, the function returns FALSE. Otherwise, it returns TRUE. Note This function performs bitwise operations on numeric inputs. Functions 636 ni.com The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: .not. (x .and. y)?
terminals.description: .not. (x .and. y)? is the logical NAND of x and y. Not And Truth Table x y .not. (x .and. y)?

entries.name: Not Or Function
entries.description: Computes the logical NOR of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are FALSE, the function returns TRUE. Otherwise, it returns FALSE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: .not. (x .or. y)?
terminals.description: .not. (x .or. y)? is the logical NOR of x and y. Not Or Truth Table x y .not. (x .or. y)?

entries.name: Not Exclusive Or Function
entries.description: Computes the logical negation of the logical exclusive or (XOR) of the inputs. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If both inputs are TRUE or both inputs are FALSE, the function returns TRUE. Otherwise, it returns FALSE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

entries.name: Implies Function
entries.description: Negates x and then computes the logical OR of y and the negated x. Both inputs must be Boolean values, numeric values, or error clusters. If x is TRUE and y is FALSE, the function returns FALSE. Otherwise, it returns TRUE. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: x .implies. y?
terminals.description: x .implies. y? is the logical OR of y and of the logical negation of x. Implies Truth Table x y x .implies. y?

entries.name: And Array Elements Function
entries.description: Returns TRUE if all the elements in Boolean array are TRUE or if Boolean array is empty. Otherwise, the function returns FALSE. This function accepts an array of any size but returns only a single value based on all values in Boolean array. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function. Functions © National Instruments 641

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array can have any number of dimensions.

terminals.name: logical AND
terminals.description: logical AND indicates if all elements in Boolean array are TRUE.

entries.name: Or Array Elements Function
entries.description: Returns FALSE if all the elements in Boolean array are FALSE or if Boolean array is empty. Otherwise, the function returns TRUE. This function accepts an array of any size but returns only a single value based on all values in Boolean array. The connector pane displays the default data types for this polymorphic function.

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array can have any number of dimensions.

terminals.name: logical OR
terminals.description: logical OR indicates if all of the elements in Boolean array are false.

entries.name: Number To Boolean Array Function
entries.description: Converts an integer or fixed-point number to a Boolean array. If you wire an integer to number, Boolean array returns an array of 8, 16, 32, or 64 elements, depending on the Functions 642 ni.com number of bits in the integer. If you wire a fixed-point number to number, the size of the array that Boolean array returns equals the word length of the fixed-point number. The 0th element of the array corresponds to the least significant bit of the two's complement representation of the integer.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number can be a number.

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array can have 8, 16, 32, or 64 elements if number is an integer. If number is a fixed- point number, Boolean array can have between 1 and 64 elements. If you wire a fixed-point number that includes an overflow status to number, this function ignores the overflow status and converts only the number to a Boolean array.

entries.name: Boolean Array To Number Function
entries.description: Converts a Boolean array to an integer or a fixed-point number by interpreting the array as the binary representation of the number. The first element of the array corresponds to the least significant bit in the number.

terminals.name: Boolean array
terminals.description: Boolean array is a one-dimensional array of Boolean values. This function truncates Boolean array if it is too long. If Boolean array is too short, this function Functions © National Instruments 643 pads Boolean array according to the Sign Extension Mode you can select by right-clicking the function.

terminals.name: Never
terminals.description: This function pads Boolean array with Boolean FALSE bits.

terminals.name: If Output Is Signed
terminals.description: (default) For an output with an unsigned representation, this function pads Boolean array with Boolean FALSE bits. For an output with a signed representation, this function pads Boolean array with the last element of the array.

terminals.name: Always
terminals.description: This function pads Boolean array with the last element of the array.

terminals.name: number
terminals.description: number is an integer or a fixed-point number. You can change the numeric representation of number on the Output Configuration page of the Properties dialog box. Right-click the function and select Properties to display the Properties dialog box. The following table shows the relationship between Boolean array and number. Boolean array Output Representation Sign Extension Mode Binary Value number {FALSE, TRUE} I8 If Output Is Signed 11111110 -2 {FALSE, TRUE} U8 If Output Is Signed 00000010 2 {FAL

terminals.name: Boolean
terminals.description: Boolean can be a scalar, an array, a cluster of Boolean values, an array of clusters of Boolean values, and so on. If Boolean is an error cluster, only the status parameter of the error cluster passes to the input terminal.
0, 1 is 0 if Boolean is FALSE and 1 if Boolean is TRUE.

entries.name: Boolean To (0,1) Function
entries.description: Converts a Boolean FALSE or TRUE value to a 16-bit integer with a value of 0 or 1, respectively. Functions 644 ni.com

terminals.name: Boolean
terminals.description: Boolean can be a scalar, an array, a cluster of Boolean values, an array of clusters of Boolean values, and so on. If Boolean is an error cluster, only the status parameter of the error cluster passes to the input terminal.
0, 1 is 0 if Boolean is FALSE and 1 if Boolean is TRUE.
